coredata.dc:description: The development of non-noble metal-based catalysts with high electrocatalytic activity and minimal poisoning for the oxidation of small organic molecules is of vital importance in the energy world. In the present work, carbon dots (CDs) and polypectin (PPC) were electrochemically synthesized and deposited on pencil graphite electrodes (PGE) to create unique multifunctional PPC/CD/PGE. Co nanoparticles (CoNPs) were subsequently electrodeposited on PPC/CDs, leading to the fabrication of CoNPs/PPC/CDs/PGE. The nanostructure nature of electrode modifier was characterized by various techniques. The electrocatalytic performance of CoNPs/PPC/CDs/PGE for the electrooxidation of formaldehyde in alkaline medium was investigated using different electrochemical methods. Based on the electrochemical results, the modified electrode showed excellent electrocatalytic activity for formaldehyde oxidation with minimum onset potential (−0.020 V/SCE) and high current density (35 mA cm−2) compared to other reported nanocatalysts based on noble (platinum group) and non-noble metals through an electrochemical-chemical (EC′) pathway. The electrochemical behavior of CoNPs/PPC/CDs/PGE in the presence of formaldehyde showed that the incorporation of CoNPs in the PPC/CD nanocomposite prepared on PGE significantly increases the electrocatalytic activity of the direct oxidation of formaldehyde, which can promise a new strategy in order to design cobalt-based high performance nanoelectrocatalysts for formaldehyde fuel cells.

(a) Chronoamperograms (at a constant potential of 0.1 V) and (b) Tafel plots of the different electrocatalysts, CoNPs/PPC/CDs /PGE, CoNPs/CDs /PGE, CoNPs/PPC/ PGE and CoNPs/PGE, in 0.1 M NaOH containing 0.3 M formaldehyde.

head.ce:title: Electrocatalytic oxidation of formaldehyde using pencil graphite electrode modified with layer-by-layer electrodeposition of carbon dots/polypectin/cobalt nanoparticles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of non-noble metal-based catalysts with high electrocatalytic activity and minimal poisoning for the oxidation of small organic molecules is of vital importance in the energy world. In the present work, carbon dots (CDs) and polypectin (PPC) were electrochemically synthesized and deposited on pencil graphite electrodes (PGE) to create unique multifunctional PPC/CD/PGE. Co nanoparticles (CoNPs) were subsequently electrodeposited on PPC/CDs, leading to the fabrication of CoNPs/PPC/CDs/PGE. The nanostructure nature of electrode modifier was characterized by various techniques. The electrocatalytic performance of CoNPs/PPC/CDs/PGE for the electrooxidation of formaldehyde in alkaline medium was investigated using different electrochemical methods. Based on the electrochemical results, the modified electrode showed excellent electrocatalytic activity for formaldehyde oxidation with minimum onset potential (−0.020 V/SCE) and high current density (35 mA cm−2) compared to other reported nanocatalysts based on noble (platinum group) and non-noble metals through an electrochemical-chemical (EC′) pathway. The electrochemical behavior of CoNPs/PPC/CDs/PGE in the presence of formaldehyde showed that the incorporation of CoNPs in the PPC/CD nanocomposite prepared on PGE significantly increases the electrocatalytic activity of the direct oxidation of formaldehyde, which can promise a new strategy in order to design cobalt-based high performance nanoelectrocatalysts for formaldehyde fuel cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, due to high electrochemical activity, easy transportation, high volume energy density, simple and cheap production from biomass, reduction of CO2, the use of carbon-based fuels, especially liquid C1 molecules including methanol, formaldehyde and formic acid in fuel cells have been considered [1–5]. Electrocatalytic oxidation of liquid C1 fuels on platinum group metals at low potentials and facilitating the conversion of substrates to CO2 makes the conventional use of noble metals in fuel cells [6,7]. One of the important challenges in the application of noble metals is the poisoning of electrode surfaces due to the production of absorbed CO, which seriously reduces the power density of fuel cells [8,9]. On the other hand, their use requires expensive costs in industrial scales. Therefore, the development of non-noble metal catalysts with high electrocatalytic activity and minimal poisoning for the oxidation of C1 molecules is of vital importance.

Among the mentioned C1 fuels, electrooxidation of formaldehyde in alkaline medium seems to be a considerable challenge due to high overpotential, poisoning of the electrode surface and slow charge transfer kinetics [10]. Electrooxidation of formaldehyde on several precious metals and their alloys including silver, gold, palladium, platinum [11–13], nickel [14], copper [15], Cu/Pt core–shell structures [16,17] and also some composite materials based on palladium or platinum (with polymers and carbon nanostructures) such as Pd-Polypyrrole [18], Pd-MWCNTs [19] and Pt-MWCNT [20] have been studied. Further and more detailed literature review shows that the basis of most of the electrodes used in the electrooxidation of formaldehyde is metal, and despite the high electrical activity, cheapness and availability of carbon electrodes, this group of bare or modified electrodes has a small contribution in this case. One of the important reason for this fact is due to the low electrocatalytic properties of carbon family electrodes towards oxidation or reduction of C1 fuels. Recently, this drawback has been solved by modification of glassy carbon electrodes with metal nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots (CDs) as the most favorable class of carbon nanomaterials [21–23]. The presence of CDs on the surface of carbon electrodes not only increases the functional groups, donation and enhances the electrocatalytic properties, but also provides a suitable surface for the stable immobilization of metal nanoparticles with minimum aggregation. In addition, the production of CO adsorbed on the electrode surface during the oxidation of carbon-based fuels is minimized and facilitates the direct oxidation to CO2.

Recently, due to the low cost, abundant materials and good catalytic activity of non-platinum nanoparticles, considerable efforts have been made to use them in the electrooxidation of formaldehyde [24–28]. A literature survey shows that the application of cobalt-based catalysts in formaldehyde oxidation reaction is limited to a few reports probably due to its poor catalytic activity. Recently, Zhu et al. reported the use of cobalt nanoparticles encapsulated in nitrogen-doped porous carbon and N-doped carbon encased metallic Co in doped carbon in formaldehyde removal [29,30]. At the same time, Rigi et al. studied the catalytic oxidation of formaldehyde using cobalt oxide nanocomposite [31]. Since the preparation of CoNPs is very simple and cost-effective, it is necessary to carry out extensive research in the field of increasing their catalytic activity, especially through the synergistic effect. To the best of our knowledge, there are no reports on the ability of CoNPs in the electrooxidation of formaldehyde in the literature.

Pectin as a natural polysaccharide with excellent properties such as biodegradability, flexibility, non-toxicity, cheapness, biocompatibility and with free hydroxyl and carboxyl groups is an excellent option for many activities in different scientific fields [32,33]. Also, gelation properties, tendency to achieve esterification reactions and complex formation, reductive behavior and anionic nature make pectin use in encapsulation, biosensors, cross-linking reaction and also in synthesis, stabilization of metal nanoparticles [32,34–36]. In addition, modified pectin through the creation of new functional groups by performing various reactions such as alkylation, oxidation, thiolation, cross-linking, grafting, polymerization, etc. leads to its application in basic science, industry and many other valuable fields [37]. In recent years, among the above reactions, pectin oxidation has received more attention due to the creation of new reactive groups and rotational freedom along the backbone through the cleavage of diol groups [38]. Therefore, considering the excellent properties of pectin in the construction of new sensors and biosensors, as well as the ability of pectin oxidation to increase new functional groups, we studied the electrochemical oxidation of pectin on a bare and CDs modified pencil graphite electrode (PGE) to create a new and efficient surface medium for the electrochemical deposition of cobalt nanoparticles. Based on our literature survey, electrochemical oxidation as well as electropolymerization of pectin has not been reported so far.

In the present work, for the first time, a new and novel cobalt-based nanocomposite produced on PGE by electrochemical layer-by-layer deposition was introduced. For this purpose, after the electrochemical synthesis and immobilization of CDs on electrode surface, the electropolymerization of pectin was studied to create polypectin (PPC) as a new, green, ecofriendly, adhesive and hydrophilic thin film for the stabilization of CoNPs. The electrochemical behavior and characteristics of CDs/PPC/CoNPs/PGE were investigated through conventional electrochemical procedures such as cyclic voltammetry (CV), chronoamperometry (CA), chronopotentiometry (CP), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), FT-IR, XRD and FE-SEM. Finally, for the first time, the ability of cobalt-based nanocomposite on PGE as a new non-platinum electrocatalyst with excellent synergestic effects of its components, without poisoning the electrode surface and with significant reduction of electron transfer resistance for the direct oxidation of formaldehyde fuel in alkaline solutions was evaluaetd.

Pectin was supplied from Sigma-Aldrich, with purity of 99.0 %. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, Merck, ≥ 99.0 %), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, Merck, 98.0 %), formaldehyde (HCHO, Merck, 37 %), cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O, Merck, 99.0 %), ethanol (C2H5OH, Merck, extra pure) and potassium chloride (KCl, Merck, ≥ 99.0 %) were used without further purification. Graphite pencil rods were purchased from local sources and their surfaces were polished on white paper to mirror-like before use. Deionized (DI) water (conductivity ≤ 6.0 μS) was employed to prepare all solutions.

All electrochemical experiments and and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed by an Autolab PGSTAT30 Potentiostat-Galvanostat (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, Netherlands) equipped with a conventional three-electrode cell including pencil graphite rod (diameter 3 mm), stainless steel sheet and saturated calomel (SC) as working, counter and reference electrodes, respectively. For two-electrodes systems, a DC power source (model PV-600, Motbaker Aryaei J., Zanjan, Iran) was used. A spectrophotometer was used to record UV–Vis spectra (T80 Double Beam UV–Visible Spectrophotometer, UK). The fluorescence spectra of all the solutions were measured with a JASCO FP-750 (Tokyo, Japan) spectrofluorometer with a 150-W xenon lamp and a 1 cm quartz cell. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM)was used to examine the surface morphology of the modified electrode (SigmaVP). Energy dispersive X-ray spectra (EDS) of the nanocomposites were performed by the use of a Philips XLS instrument (The Netherlands) at room temperature.

CDs was electrochemically synthesized according to procedure reported by Liu et al. with a little modification [39]. Briefly, two parallel graphite electrodes with a distance of 7 cm immersed in an electrolyte solution containing 35 mL of ethanol, 8 mL of water and 0.03 g of NaOH were connected to a DC power source and a voltage of 5 V was applied for 3 h under nitrogen. The resulted yellow solution was centrifuged to obtain 500μg mL‐1 CDs solution.

Electrical connections with copper wires to the external part of PGEs were used in electrochemical studies. The side surface of the pencil was covered by a Teflon tape, and the cross-sectional area of PGE was only exposed to the electrolyte solution. Before each experiment, the surface of the electrode was polished on white paper to make it mirror-like and smooth. Due to the softness of the PGE surface compared to traditional metal surfaces, it was easily polished in one minute and the resulting polished surface of the electrode was cleaned using ultrasound in distilled water. To prepare CDs/PGE, the as-prepared PGE (as working electrode) was immersed in 10 mL of CDs solution and 10 potential cycles were performed from −1.0 to 1.0 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 using a three-electrode system [23]. Next, after several washing of CDs/PGE with deionized water, polymerization of pectin on CD/PGE was performed by cyclic voltammetry technique on a solution containing H2SO4 (0.1 M) + 1 mM pectin for 10 cycles in the −0.6–1.1 V potential range at scan rate of 20 mV s−1. Finally, chronoamperometry at potential of −1.0 V vs. SCE for 300 s in a solution containing 0.50 M CoCl2·6H2O and 0.10 M KCl was employed for electrodeposition of CoNPs on PPC/CDs/PGE. Fig. 1 shows a graphical representation of the proposed method in the preparation of CoNPs/PPC/CD/PGE.

The UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of the electrochemical synthesized CDs are shown in Fig. S1(a) (Supplementary materials). As can be seen, CDs show two absorption peaks at around 240 and 360 nm that related to the π-π* transitions of the aromatic CC sp2 bond and π-π* transitions of the carbonyl functional groups [40,41]. On the other hand, the presence of an emission peak at roughly 480 nm at excitation wavelength of 340 nm validates the optical characteristics of CDs. The images of electrochemically synthesized CDs (Fig. S1(b), Supplementary Materials) shows that they are yellow under sunlight and green under a 340 nm UV lamp irradiation. These observations confirm the successful synthesis of CDs through the electrochemical method.

Also, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. S2, Supplementary Materials) were used to detect the presence of different functional groups in the prepared CDs. The appearance of a broad absorption peak between 3500–3600 cm−1 and a stretching vibration band of 1550–1650 cm−1 respectively indicates the presence of hydroxyl and CO groups related to –COOH in CDs structure. In addition, CO stretching vibrational bands at 1000–1100 and 700–800 cm−1 confirm the presence of carboxylic acid and other functional groups containing oxygen [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization the modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned in the experimental section, sequential cyclic voltammetry technique was used for electrodeposition of CDs on PGE. Fig. 2 a shows the voltammograms recorded during the immobilization of CDs on PGE at a potential scan rate of 50 mV s−1. As can be seen, the peak currents increase after each potential scan, which indicates the deposition of the layer on the electrode surface. The appearance of a well-defined pair of redox peaks (a1 and c1) during the electrodepostion process can be related to the oxidation and reduction of oxygenated functional groups on CDs [43]. The absence of new peaks during the electrodeposition process shows the formation of a stable layer on electrode surface under the applied conditions. The field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) image of PGE/CDs (Fig. 2b) shows the formation of a tight and uniform layer on the electrode surface.

In next step, the electrooxidation of pectin was performed in 0.1 M sulfuric acid on bare PGE and CDs/PGE. The acidic solution hydrolyzes the pectin and also provides the protons needed for the oxidation process [37]. Cyclic voltammetry at different potential ranges and different potential scan rates was used for the electropolymerization of pectin (PC) on PG and CDs/PG electrodes to create a green polymer film, polypectin (PPC), on the target electrodes. The obtained experimental results showed that the optimal potential range for the formation of a stable polymer with high surface adhesion behavior is at a voltage of −0.6 to 1.1 V vs. SCE with a potential scan rate of 0.01 V/s. Fig. 3 shows the sequential cyclic voltammograms of CDs/PGE in 1 mM PC + 0.1 M H2SO4 (solid lines). The dashed voltammogram is the first scan of electrooxidation of PC on bare graphite electrode. As can be seen, there are three anodic peaks (a1, a2 and a3) and two related cathodic peaks (c2 and c1) in the reverse scan. The sequential cyclic voltammograms (Fig. 3, solid lines) of PC electropolymerization on CDs/PGE also show three anodic peaks (a1, a2 and a3) and two well-defined cathodic peaks (c1 and c2). As can be seen, with the increase of potential cycles, only the intensity of peaks a2 and c2 increases and the intensity of the anodic peak at 0.88 V (a3) ​​decreases, which confirms the growth of the polymer layer and the formation of a uniform deep adhesive coating on the surface of the electrode. The lack of progressive polymer layer formation on the bare graphite electrode during successive cycles confirms the importance of the CD layer prior to PC polymerization (CVs not shown). The surface groups, structure and components of PPC/CD and pure PC were thoroughly investigated using FTIR spectroscopy. The recorded FTIR spectra (Fig. S3, Supplementary Materials) show a significant decrease in the intensity of PPC compared to pure PC in the entire wavenumber range, especially in the range of 4000–3000 cm−1, which is attributed to the OH stretching vibration mode. This fact confirms the participation of hydroxyl groups in the formation of new functional groups as well as the reduction of free OH groups in the PC backbone after the polymerization process [44]. Furthermore, the spectrum of PPC/CD composites shows a weak asymmetric vibration of the carboxylic group at 1636 cm−1 as well as a broad shoulder near 2600 cm−1 corresponding to the OH stretching vibration of dimerized carboxylate groups in the composite with hydrogen bonds [45].

In the final step, cobalt nanoparticles (CoNPs) were electrochemically incorporated on the surface of PPC/CDs/PGE to prepare a new class of modified electrodes based on cobalt nanocomposites. In order to determine the surface morphology of the prepared electrodes, their FESEM images were recorded step by step (Fig. S4, Supplementary Materials). As can be seen from Fig. S4-a), while the formation of PPC on bare graphite is unsuccessful, after covering the surface with a thin layer of CDs (Fig. S4-b), a dimensional and uniform layer of pectin polymer in the form of ordered sequence of fibers are deposited on the surface of the electrode (Fig. S4-c). The entire assembly on the electrode surface (CoNPs/PPC/CD) can be seen in Fig. S4(d), where PPC is dispersed on CDs layer containing metal nanoparticles in the polymer phase. Fig. S5 (Supplementary Materials) illustrates the energy dispersive X-ray spectra (EDS) of the modifiers and the acquired findings. As can be seen, the appearance of specific regions of cobalt atoms in the CoNPs/PPC/CDs film (Fig. S5-d) confirmed the deposition of Co particles in PGE. In addition, the surface of PPC/CDs/PGE (Fig. S5-c), CDs/PGE (Fig. S5-b) and PPC/PGE (Fig. S5-a) films show specific regions of carbon and oxygen atoms of the polymer layer composed of PPC and CDs. Moreover, elemental mapping analysis was used to obtain more information about the types of elements present, the amounts of each element and the distribution of elements on the surface of the modifiers (Fig. S6, Supplementary Materials). The obtained results are completely in accordance with the results of FESEM and EDX. Fig. S6(d) exhibits the elemental mapping of Co, O, and C atoms from CoNPs, PPC, and CDs electrodeposited on the PPC/CDs/PGE electrode surface, which confirms that CoNPs are uniformly distributed on the polymeric composite layer.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique was used to investigate the crystal phase structure of the prepared Co/PPC/CDs/PGE electrocatalyst (Fig. 4 ). As can be seen, the XRD pattern of PPC shows a broad peak in the 2θ range of 10-20° indicating an amorphous structure [46]. The XRD pattern of CDs exhibits a peak at approximately 2θ = 26.6°, which corresponds to the (002) plane of carbon [47]. The intensity decrease of the C peak along with an increase in its full width at half maximum (FWHM) in CDs/PGE in comparison with C peak in XRD pattern of PGE (inset of Fig. 4) confirms the deposition of CDs on PGE as well as the formation of oxygen functional groups on electrode surface [48]. This change is attributed to the small size of the prepared CDs [48]. Finally, the observed four distinct peaks at Bragg angle values of 44-45° (002), 47-48° (011), 63-65° (012), and 75-76° (110) are corresponded to a hexagonal close-packed (hcp) structure of Co nanoparticles [49]. The calculated average size of Co crystallites and CDs in Co/PPC/CDs/PGE nanocomposites using the Debye-Scherrer equation were 78.01 nm and 39.01 nm, respectively [50]. It must be noted that the peak at 2θ = 55° corresponds to carbon (006) of the support material (PGE).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of CoNPs based electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The incorporation of CoNPs onto the modified electrodes was further confirmed by monitoring their cyclic voltammograms in an alkaline solution. Fig. 5 shows the recorded cyclic voltammograms of the prepared electrodes including CoNPs/PPC/CDs/PGE, CoNPs/CDs/PGE, CoNPs/PPC/PGE and CoNPs/PGE in 0.1 M NaOH solution at a potential scan rate of 20 mV s−1 and in the potential range of −0.5–0.8 V. As can be seen, similar to the pervious reported works [51–53], the voltammograms contain two anodic peaks at 0.29 V (peak a1) and 0.51 V (peak a2) related to Co(II)/Co(III) and Co(III)/Co(IV), respectively. Accordingly, two well-defined cathodic peaks c1 and c2 located at 0.12 and 0.48 V can be assigned to the reduction of cobalt species produced in the forward scan. The electrochemical properties of all electrodes based on CoNPs are almost similar except that the CV profile of CoNPs/PPC/CDs/PGE (Fig. 5, curve d) shows larger anodic and cathodic peak current densities. These findings suggest that the incorporation of thin film sublayers PPC and CDs into the structure of the CoNPs/PPC/CDs/PGE promoted the redox activity of CoNPs. In addition, the results show that the presence of PPC/CDs layers with specific surface area and nanodimensions in CoNPs/PPC/CDs/PGE provides more electrochemically active sites for the cobalt-based electrocatalytic process compared to CoNPs/PGE.

The effect of potential scan rates on the voltammograms obtained from CoNPs/PPC/CD/PGE in 0.1 M NaOH solution is presented in Fig. S7 (Supplementary Materials). A significant shift in anodic and cathodic peak potentials (for a1 and c1) with increasing scan rate from 10 to 70 mV s−1 confirmed the electron transfer limitation of the electrochemical process [23]. Also, as seen in inset of Fig. S7, the appearance of a linear relationship between the square root of the potential scan rate (υ1/2) and the cathodic and anodic peak current density (Jp) indicates the currents controlled by the diffusion of hydroxide ions.

The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of an electrode refers to the accessible surface area involved in electrochemical reactions, which can be determined using the double layer capacitance (Cdl), specific capacitance (Cs) and Eq. (1) [54].
(1)
ECSA = C d l / C s
The ECSA values ​​of the prepared CoNPs-based electrocatalysts are summarized in Table 1 . As can be seen, the ECSA of PGE modified only with CoNPs is smaller than that of other electrodes, which is probably due to the low electrodeposition of CoNPs on PGEs without PPC or CD sublayers. Also, the results show that while the ECSA of PGE is increased by separate immobilizatıon of CD or PPC layers, the presence of the assembled bilayers provides a suitable surface for the deposition of large amounts of CoNPs, leading to an increase in the ECSA of ConNPs/PPC/CDs/PGE to 0.56 ± 0.05 cm2.

The electrochemical oxidation of formaldehyde (0.3 M) in alkaline medium was evaluated on the surface of modified and unmodified PGEs. Fig. 6 shows the obtained cyclic voltammogramms in 0.1 M NaOH at the various electrodes in the absence and presence of formaldehyde. As can be seen the irreversible oxidation of formaldehyde at bare PGE starts at about 0.6 V and reaches a small current density at 0.7 V (Fig. 6a). After modification of PGE with CDs (Fig. 6b), the electrooxidation potential of formaldehyde shits to less positive potential (about 0.50 V) with an increase in current density probably due to mild electrocatalytic properties of CDs layer. The electrocatlaytic effect of CoNPs on the oxidation of formaldehyde is shown by cyclic voltammograms depicted by Fig. 6 (c-f). As seen the presence of CDs and polypectin (PPC) sublayers significantly altered the electrocatalytic effect of CoNPs, so that the oxidation of formaldehyde in CoNPs/PGE (Fig. 6c) is nothing sense. The CoNPs incorporated to CDs layer (CoNPs /CDs/PGE) (Fig. 6e) enhances the oxidation of formaldehude in comparison to PPC layer (CoNPs /PPC/PGE) (Fig. 6d) due to electrocatalytic synergistic effect between CoNPs and CDs. This fact is completely confirmed by increase in current density as well as shifting anodic potential peak to more less positive values. These findings suggested that CoNPs were primarily responsible for the catalytic oxidation of formaldehyde. However, the presence of CDs and PPC components along with CoNPs were vital to the development of formaldehyde oxidation as can be seen in Fig. 6f. In practice, the CDs/PPC combination not only offered a high surface area to raise the number of CoNPs through reducing the dimension of the electrodeposited CoNPs, but also stabilize them on PPC sublayer as monitored in Fig. 5d. The small CoNPs high surface-to-volume ratio resulted in a higher overall electroactive surface area, which increased the possibility of substrate interaction. As a result, and based on the significant increase of anodic current density along with the disappearance of cathodic peaks of CoNPs in the presence of formaldehyde (Fig. 6f), it can be stated that formaldehyde is electrocatalytically oxidized through EC′ mechanism [55]. In this process, Co(II) mediator is oxidized to Co(III), followed by Co(III) species oxidized formaldehyde and Co(II) species regeneration as presented in Fig. 7 .

For further investigations, the electrocatalytic behavior of CoNPs/PPC/CD/PGE at different concentration of formaldehyde was evaluated by cyclic voltammetry. Fig. 8 (a) depicts the cyclic voltammograms in the concentration range of 0.1 M to 0.3 M (in 0.1 M NaOH) at a potential scan rate of 20 mV s−1. As shown, the anodic peak current density increases with increasing formaldehyde concentration up to 0.3 M (inset of Fig. 8a). On the other hand, no significant increase in anodic peak intensity was observed at concentrations greater than 0.3 M (data not shown). These results indicated that there are no sufficient CoNPs active catalyst sites for the electrocatalytic oxidation of a large amount of formaldehyde [24]. Moreover, the gradual decrease in the intensity of cathodic peaks intensity with increasing the formaldehyde concentrations and their complete disappearance at high concentrations (≥0.3 M) once more confirmed the proposed EC′ mechanism.

The relationship between Jp/υ1/2 and the potential scan rate is used to confirm a variety of mechanisms that the electrochemical step is accompanied by a following or proceding chemical reaction [56]. To obtain Jp/υ1/2 values, cyclic voltammograms of CoNPs/PPC/CD/PGE were recorded at different potential scan rates in 0.1 M NaOH containing 0.3 M formaldehyde (high concentration of substrate)) (Fig. 8b). As can be seen, the values ​​of Jpa/υ1/2 (top inset in Fig. 8b) decrease with increasing scan rate of the potential and remain almost constant at high scan rates. This fact shows the catalytic nature of the currents at low potential scan rates, which change to diffusion currents at high scans [56], which fully confirms the EC' mechanism for the electrooxidation of formaldehyde using proposed electrode. At low scan rates, there is enough time for the chemical oxidation of formaldehyde by the catalyst (Co(III)) and the regeneration of its reduced form (Co(II)) on the electrode surface. The decrease in current with increasing scan rate limits the chemical catalytic reaction, and finally the values ​​of Jpa/υ1/2 reach constant values ​​at high scan rates, which is known as the pure diffusion zone. Accordingly, increasing Jpc/Jpa ratio values by increasing potential scan rates confirms the presence of following catalytic chemical reaction coupled with electrochemical step (down inset in Fig. 8b).

It is well known that the oxidation of formaldehyde on solid metal electrodes or metal modified solid electrodes proceeds from direct oxidation to CO2 or an indirect pathway via intermediate formation (Fig. S8, Supplementary Materials) [57,58]. Therefore, in most cyclic voltammograms, two anodic peaks can be observed, one peak in the forward potential scan attributed to the direct/indirect oxidation of formaldehyde and the other peak in the reverse scan resulting from the desorption of species or incomplete carbon dioxide in the forward scan. In the present work, the appearance of an anodic peak about 0.6 V via the direct electooxidation of formaldehyde on unmodified PGE (bare PGE) (Fig. 6a, curve b) as well as an anodic peak at 0.3 V attributed to the catalytic oxidation of formaldehyde on modified PGE (CoNPs/PPC/CDs/PGE) (Fig. 6f, curve b) confirms the complete oxidation of formaldehyde through the production of CO2. Therefore, it can be stated that the absence of CO residues from incomplete oxidation and other mediators did not cause poisoning of the electrode surface. This unique behavior enables the application of the proposed CoNPs/PPC/CDs/PGE in formaldehyde fuel cells.

The electrocatalysts long-term stability of CoNPs/PPC/CDs and generation of some poisoning particles was evaluated using chronoamperometry as a reliable and effective electrochemical technique [59]. Typical chronoamperograms recorded in 0.1 M NaOH solution containing 0.3 M formaldehyde at a constant potential of 0.1 V for the synthesized CoNPs-based modifiers on PGEs are shown in Fig. 9 (a). As can be seen, the current density resulting from the electrocatalytic oxidation of formaldehyde on CoNPs/PPC/CDs/PGE is significantly larger than the other tested electrodes, which is probably related to the high density of CoNPs embedded in the PPC matrix containing CDs. Also, the presence of many oxygenated groups on CDs can prevent the production of CO as a poison of the electrode surface [23].

The electrocatalytic activities of various prepared modifier containing CoNPs for formaldehyde oxidation were investigated based on Tafel polarization plots. The kinetic parameters, especially the anodic Tafel slope for formaldehyde oxidation on the surfaces of electrocatalysts, were determined from the forward scan of the cyclic voltammograms presented in Fig. 6(c-f) and based on the following Tafel equation (Eq. (3) [60]:
(3)
η = b log
J
+ a
where, η, b and J are the overpotential, Tafel slope and current density, respectively.

The experimentally determined kinetic parameters are presented in Table 2 . The obtained Tafel plots for the oxidation of formaldehyde using the CoNPs based layers on PGE showed slopes of 320, 336, 338 and 346 mV dec-1 for CoNPs /PPC/CDs/PGE, CoNPs /CDs/PGE, CoNPs /PPC/PGE and CoNPs/PGE, respectively (Fig. 9b). The lower Tafel slope for CoNPs /PPC/CDs as well as Eonset in comparison with the other CoNPs based layers once more clearly confirmed its less overpotential and more electrocatalytic activity.

To further investigations, the electrocatalytic characteristics of synthezied nanocompounds was evaluated by chronopotentiometry (CP) technique. Fig. 10 (a) displays the recorded choronopotentiograms of the electrocatalysts at a constant current density of 20 mA cm−2 for the electrooxidation of formaldehyde. As can be seen, CoNPs/PPC/CD/PGE (curve a) has the lowest potential for formaldehyde electrooxidation compared to the CoNPs/PPC/PGE (curve b), CoNPs/CD/PGE (curve c), and CoNPs/PGE (curve d) which is probably related to the reduction of the potential of electrocatalytic polarization and the reduction of sensitivity to poisoning. In contrast, the gradual increase in polarization potential over time suggests an escalation in the effects of poisoning as the adsorption of by-product species hampers access to electrocatalytic sites. It must be mentioned that the increase in polarization potential of all electrocatalysts continue untile the end of fuel electrooxidation activity and transition time appears with the initiation of the oxygen evolution reaction. The higher transition time obtained for CoNPs/PPC/CD/PGE confirms the successful deposition of CoNPs in the electrode matrix compared to other tested Co-based electrocatalysts.Throughout the process of fuel oxidation, toxic species, particularly COads, contaminate the surface of Co-based electrocatalysts, limiting further fuel oxidation [61].

The electrocatalytic activity of the proposed CoNPs/PPC/CD/PGE for the electrooxidation of formaldehyde was evaluated by comparing it with other electrodes reported in the literature and under conditions similar to the present work for further investigation [62–67,27,28] (Table 3 ). The negative onset potential and high current density at low potential scan rates demonstrated that the electrocatalytic activity of CoNPs/PPC/CDs/PGE to formaldehyde oxidation is comparable or superior to that of other reported electrocatalysts.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to characterize the surface electrical properties and electrochemical performance of four modified electrodes based on CoNPs [68]. For this purpose, their Nyquist plots were recorded in solution containing 0.1 M NaOH, 0.3 M formaldehyde in the frequency range of 0.1 Hz to 10 kHz, amplitude 10 mV, and constant bias potential 0.75 V (Fig. 10b). The values ​​of the diameter of the semicircle of the graphs in the high frequency section indicate the electron transfer resistance (Rct) on the electrode surface, which is related to the kinetics of the electrochemical reactions occurring on the electrode surface. Accordingly, the almost vertical line along the hypothetical axis at low frequencies (Warburg line) represents the capacitive behavior related to the double layer capacitance and diffusion processes at the electrode–electrolyte interface. Furthermore, the intercept of the x-axis at the beginning of the EIS curve represents the equivalent series resistance (Rs), which includes contributions from the bulk resistance of the electrolyte, contact resistance, and any other resistive elements in the electrochemical system. Rs is an important parameter that reflects the overall resistance to electron flow within the electrochemical cell.

According to Fig. 10b, the presence of PPC and CDs along with CoNPs on PGE (curve a) significantly reduces the electron transfer resistance compared to other modified PGEs (curves b-d) as well as bare PGE (curve e), which is probably due to the high electrocatalytic performance of CoNPs in the substrate. These observations once more confirm the manufacture of a suitable layer from coupling PPC with CDs on PGE surface for incorporation of CoNPs as well as their overall synergistic effects in mediator. This fact is evident from the comparison of the curves obtained from the binary composite prepared from CoNPs-CDs (curve b), CoNPs-PPC (curve c) and bare CoNPs (curve d) on the PGE surface in Fig. 10b. As can be seen, the presence of CDs (as a highly conductive material) along with CoNPs provides a more effective medium for the easy electron transfer pathway and diffusion process at the electrode–electrolyte interface than bare PPC. Overall, the very low Rct values obtained with PGE modification indicate that the CoNPs and PPC/CDs films adhere effectively to the PGE surface. These observations confirm the acceptable agreement of EIS findings with the results of CV studies. Finally, it was demonstrated that experimental results for the all modifiers surface analysis for electrocatlytic oxidation of formaldehyde were fitted to the common Randles equivalent circuit (inset of Fig. 10b). The EIS fitting parameters including Rs, Rct and CPE obtained from parallel measurements (n = 3) using the selected equivalent circuit are summarized in Table 4 .

In the present work, the application of CoNPs/PPC/CDs modified PGE for the formaldehyde fuel cells is introduced as a versatile candidate with significant electrocatalytic activity. For the first time, PC is electrochemically polymerized on PGE using CV technique after CDs film coating. The PPC/CDs sublayer on PGE shows a novel green media for the the electrochemical deposition of CoNPs on electrode with high tendency. As a general result, CDs by creating functional groups increase the active surface of the electrode and electron transfer and facilitate conduction. On the other hand, PPC with its biocompatible nature and as a hydrophilic adhesive layer, in addition to stabilizing nanoparticles, can cause their uniform distribution on the electrode surface. The electrocatalytic properties of the CoNPs/PPC/CDs/PGE toward formaldehyde oxidation were then studied using cyclic voltammetry and chronoamperometry techniques. The significant increase in the catalytic activity of CoNPs/PPC/CDs/PGE compared to CoNPs/CDs/PGE, ConPs/PPC/PGE, and CoNPs/PGE may be attributed to the synergistic effect of CDs and PPC with CoNPs. Evaluation of formaldehyde oxidation process on CoNPs/PPC/CDs/PGE indicates that direct oxidation through EC' mechanism is predominant and the CO poisoning process has not occurred. Finally, the Tafel plots results and EIS investigations confirmed an improved electrocatalytic performance of the CoNPs/PPC/CDs nanocomposite in formaldehyde oxidation on PGE.
